name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا لُقْمَـٰنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (١٢)
```

```english
Certainly We gave Luqman wisdom, saying, ‘Give thanks to Allah; and whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful, [let him know that] Allah is indeed all-sufficient, all-laudable.’ (12)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَـٰنُ لِٱبْنِهِۦ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُۥ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٣)
```

```english
When Luqman said to his son, as he advised him: ‘O my son! Do not ascribe any partners to Allah. Polytheism is indeed a great injustice.’ (13)
```

```arabic
وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ فِى عَامَيْنِ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَيْكَ إِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٤)
```

```english
We have enjoined man concerning his parents: His mother carried him through weakness upon weakness, and his weaning takes two years. Give thanks to Me and to your parents. To Me is the return. (14)
```

```arabic
وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُشْرِكَ بِى مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا ۖ وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا ۖ وَٱتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَىَّ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. Keep their company honourably in this world and follow the way of those who turn to Me penitently. Then to Me will be your return, whereat I will inform you concerning what you used to do. (15)
```

```arabic
يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنَّهَآ إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُن فِى صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَوْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ (١٦)
```

```english
‘O my son! Even if it should be the weight of a mustard seed, and [even though] it should be in a rock, or in the heavens, or in the earth, Allah will produce it. Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware. (16)
```

```arabic
يَـٰبُنَىَّ أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱنْهَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَكَ ۖ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (١٧)
```

```english
O my son! Maintain the prayer and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and be patient through whatever may befall you. That is indeed the steadiest of courses. (17)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ (١٨)
```

```english
Do not turn your cheek away disdainfully from the people, and do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed Allah does not like any swaggering braggart. (18)
```

```arabic
وَٱقْصِدْ فِى مَشْيِكَ وَٱغْضُضْ مِن صَوْتِكَ ۚ إِنَّ أَنكَرَ ٱلْأَصْوَٰتِ لَصَوْتُ ٱلْحَمِيرِ (١٩)
```

```english
Be modest in your bearing, and lower your voice. Indeed the ungainliest of voices is the donkey’s voice.’ (19)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses contain an allusion to the granting of wisdom to Luqman and a selection of his maxims and exhortations to his son. He is not mentioned in the Qur’an except in this Surah. This placement is appropriate as it contrasts his story, which is filled with wisdom (الحكمة) and exhortation (الموعظة), with the account of those who purchase idle talk (لهو الحديث) to mislead others from the path of Allah without knowledge and take it in mockery.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا لُقْمَانَ الْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ** — "Certainly We gave Luqman wisdom, [saying], ‘Give thanks to Allah’" (31:12). Wisdom (الحكمة) — *the beneficial cognitive knowledge that represents the middle path of moderation between ignorance and cunning* — is understood from its various usages. Regarding His saying: **أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي** (Give thanks to Me), it has been said that it implies a suppressed command, meaning: "And We said: 'Give thanks to Me.'"

However, it appears more likely that it is an explanation of the granting of wisdom itself without a suppressed command. This is because the reality of gratitude (الشكر) is to place blessings in their proper place in a manner that points to the beneficence of the Benefactor. Performing this as is its right depends on knowing the Benefactor and knowing His blessings as blessings, and knowing how to place them correctly so they reflect His beneficence. Thus, the granting of wisdom was an impetus for him to give thanks; therefore, the granting of wisdom is a command to give thanks by implication.

In His saying: **أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ** (Give thanks to Allah), there is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural to the third person. This is because when the Speaker uses the first-person plural, it is to manifest greatness by speaking on behalf of Himself and His servants. Saying "Give thanks to Us" in this context would not suit the monotheism (التوحيد) required in gratitude, which is evident.

His saying: **وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ** — "Whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful, then Allah is indeed all-sufficient, all-laudable" (31:12). This expresses His independence, the Exalted, as the benefit of gratitude returns only to the soul of the grateful one, and ingratitude (الكفر) only harms the soul itself, not Him, the Glorified. Whoever gives thanks does so for his own benefit, and Allah, the Glorified, does not benefit from it due to His absolute self-sufficiency (الغنى المطلق). Whoever disbelieves only harms himself; indeed, Allah is all-sufficient (غني) — gratitude does not affect Him with benefit nor harm — and all-laudable (حميد) — praised for what He has bestowed, whether one gives thanks or is ungrateful.

The use of the present tense for gratitude, indicating continuity, and the past tense for ingratitude, indicating a single instance, suggests that gratitude only benefits if it is continuous, whereas ingratitude causes harm even if it occurs once.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَانُ لِابْنِهِ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُ يَا بُنَيَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ** — "When Luqman said to his son, as he was advising him: ‘O my son! Do not ascribe any partners to Allah. Polytheism is indeed a great injustice’" (31:13). The greatness of any act is measured by the greatness of its effect, and the greatness of a sin is measured by the greatness of the One disobeyed. Since the punishment of the Great One is great, the greatest of sins is the disobedience of Allah, for His greatness and majesty are above all greatness and majesty, in that He is Allah, having no partner. Thus, the greatest of His disobediences is to deny that He is Allah, without partner.

His saying: **إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ** (Polytheism is indeed a great injustice), by leaving "greatness" absolute without restricting it by comparison to other sins, indicates that it possesses a degree of greatness that cannot be measured.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنسَانَ بِوَالِدَيْهِ** — "We have enjoined man concerning his parents..." (31:14) until the end of the verse. This is a parenthetical statement occurring within the speech quoted from Luqman; it is not from the speech of Luqman himself. It is inserted here to indicate that the obligation of thanking one's parents is like the obligation of thanking Allah. Rather, it is part of thanking Him, the Exalted, because it stems from His testament and command. Thus, thanking them is an act of worship (العبادة) to Him, and His worship is gratitude.

His saying: **حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ وَهْنًا عَلَى وَهْنٍ وَفِصَالُهُ فِي عَامَيْنِ** — "his mother carried him through weakness upon weakness, and his weaning takes two years" (31:14). This mentions some of the hardship and suffering his mother endured during pregnancy and upbringing to motivate him to thank them, especially the mother.

Weakness (الوهن) means frailty (الضعف). It is either a circumstantial qualifier (حال) meaning "possessing weakness," or a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) where the implied meaning is "she weakened with weakness upon weakness." Weaning (الفصال) refers to stopping breastfeeding. The meaning of the weaning being in two years is its completion by the end of two years, which implies that the period of breastfeeding is two years. When this is joined with **His saying, the Exalted**: **وَحَمْلُهُ وَفِصَالُهُ ثَلَاثُونَ شَهْرًا** — "and his gestation and weaning take thirty months" (46:15), there remain six months for the minimum period of pregnancy, an allusion that will be repeated later.

His saying: **أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيْكَ إِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ** — "[saying], ‘Give thanks to Me and to your parents. To Me is the return’" (31:14). This is an explanation of His saying: **وَصَّيْنَا** (We have enjoined) at the beginning of the verse. That is, Our testament was Our command to thank them just as We commanded him to thank Allah. His saying: **إِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ** (To Me is the return) is a warning and an emphasis on the command to give thanks.

The discussion regarding the transition (الالتفات) in this verse in His saying: **أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيْكَ إِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ** from the first-person plural to the first-person singular is the same as the discussion regarding the transition in the previous saying: **أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ**.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِن جَاهَدَاكَ عَلَى أَن تُشْرِكَ بِي مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا** — "But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them..." (31:15) until the end of the verse. That is, if they persist in striving against you to make that which you have no knowledge of—or no knowledge of its reality—a partner to Me, then do not obey them and do not associate anything with Me. The meaning of the supposed partner being something of which there is "no knowledge" is that it is non-existent and absolutely unknown, such that no knowledge can pertain to it. Thus, the meaning results in: "Do not associate with Me that which is nothing." This is the summary of what was mentioned in *al-Kashshaf*, and it is perhaps supported by **His saying, the Exalted**: **أَتُنَبِّئُونَ اللَّهَ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَلَا فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?" (10:18).

It has also been said that "to associate" (تشرك) means to disbelieve, and "what" (ما) means "that which," so the meaning is: "If they strive against you to disbelieve in Me with a disbelief for which you have no proof, then do not obey them." This is supported by the repeated negation of any authority (سلطان) for the partner in His speech, such as His saying: **مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِ إِلَّا أَسْمَاءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَا أَنتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُم مَّا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَانٍ** — "You do not worship besides Him but [mere] names that you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority" (12:40), among other verses.

His saying: **وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِي الدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا وَاتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَيَّ** — "keep their company honorably in this world and follow the way of him who turns to Me in penitence" (31:15). These two phrases are like a summary and clarification of what preceded in the two verses regarding the testament concerning them and the prohibition of obeying them if they strive for polytheism (الشرك) in Allah.

The Glorified says: It is obligatory for man to accompany them in worldly matters—other than religion, which is the path of Allah—with honorable companionship (صحاب معروف) and conventional social interaction that is not reprehensible, by observing their condition with kindness and gentleness without alienation or harshness, and by bearing the hardships that come from them. For the world is but a few passing days. As for the parents, if they are among those who turn to Allah, then follow their path; otherwise, follow the path of others who turn to Allah.

From here it becomes clear that in His saying: **وَاتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَيَّ** (and follow the way of him who turns to Me in penitence), there is a subtle conciseness. It implies that if they were among those who turn to Allah, then follow their path; otherwise, do not obey them and follow the path of others who turn to Allah.

His saying: **ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "Then to Me will be your return, whereat I will inform you concerning what you used to do" (31:15). That is, what has been mentioned is your duty in the world, then you shall return to Me on the Day of Resurrection, and I will manifest to you the reality of your deeds that you performed in the world, and I will judge between you according to what your deeds necessitate of good or evil.

From what has passed, it is clear that His saying: **فِي الدُّنْيَا** (in this world) signifies, firstly, the restriction of honorable companionship to worldly matters rather than religious ones; secondly, the belittling of the matter of companionship, as it is only for a few days, so there is no great harm in bearing the hardships of serving them; and thirdly, the contrast with the Day of Return to Allah alluded to in His saying: **ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ** (Then to Me will be your return).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يَا بُنَيَّ إِنَّهَا إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُن فِي صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ أَوْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا اللَّهُ** — "‘O my son! Even if it should be the weight of a mustard seed, and [even if] it should be in a rock, or in the heavens, or in the earth, Allah will produce it...’" (31:16). They mentioned that the pronoun in "it" (إنها) refers to the deed (الخصلة) of good or evil, as indicated by the context. It is also the subject of "be" (كان), and "the weight of a seed" (مثقال حبة) is its predicate. The meaning of it being "in a rock" is its concealment by being settled inside a solid rock, or in the heavens, or in the earth. The meaning of "producing it" is bringing it forth for reckoning and recompense.

The previous section of his quoted speech pertained to monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of partners, and what is in this verse is a second section regarding the Return (المعاد) and the reckoning of deeds. The meaning is: O my son, if the deed you performed, whether good or evil, be the lightest and most minute of things, like the weight of a mustard seed, and that small deed be settled in the heart of a rock or in any place in the heavens and the earth, Allah will produce it for reckoning and recompense. For Allah is All-Attentive (لطيف) — His knowledge penetrates the depths of things and reaches every hidden thing — and All-Aware (خبير) — knowing the essence of all existents.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يَا بُنَيَّ أَقِمِ الصَّلَاةَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَانْهَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَاصْبِرْ عَلَى مَا أَصَابَكَ إِنَّ ذَلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** — "‘O my son! Maintain the prayer and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and be patient through whatever may visit you. That is indeed [a matter] of determination’" (31:17). This verse and those following it from his speech pertain to a selection of deeds and virtuous ethics.

Among the deeds is the prayer (الصلاة), which is the pillar of religion, followed by bidding what is right (الأمر بالمعروف) and forbidding what is wrong (النهي عن المنكر). Among the ethics is patience (الصبر) through whatever calamity may strike.

His saying: **إِنَّ ذَلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** (That is indeed [a matter] of determination). The demonstrative "that" (ذلك) refers to patience, and the distant demonstrative is for glorification and elevation. The view of some that the demonstrative refers to all that preceded—prayer, bidding what is right, forbidding what is wrong, and patience—is misplaced, because patience is repeatedly counted among the "matters of determination" (عزم الأمور) in His speech, such as His saying: **وَلَمَن صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذَلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** — "But whoever is patient and forgives—that is indeed [a matter] of determination" (42:43), and His saying: **إِن تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ ذَلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** — "if you are patient and God-conscious, that is indeed [a matter] of determination" (3:186).

Determination (العزم) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is the heart's resolve to carry out a matter. Patience (الصبر) — which is the soul's self-restraint in a matter — being part of determination is because the heart's resolve remains firm as long as it is not dissolved or broken. Thus, patience is a necessary concomitant of seriousness in resolve and its preservation, and it is part of the soul's power and fortitude.

The view of some that the meaning is that "that is from Allah's decree (عزيمة) and His obligation in matters" is far-fetched, as is the view of some that "determination" (العزم) means "decisiveness" (الجزم) and that it is the dialect of Hudhayl.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ** — "‘Do not turn your cheek away from people in disdain, and do not walk exultantly on the earth; indeed Allah does not like any swaggering braggart’" (31:18). Al-Raghib said: *al-Sa‘ar* (الصعر) is a tilt in the neck, and *al-Tas‘ir* (التصعير) is tilting it away from looking out of pride. He said: **وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ**. And he said: *al-Marah* (المرح) is excessive joy and expansion in it. [End of quote].

The meaning is: Do not turn your face away from people out of arrogance, and do not walk on the earth with the gait of one whose joy is excessive. Indeed, Allah does not love anyone who is overtaken by vanity (الخيلاء) — which is arrogance (التكبر) by imagining a virtue (تخيل الفضيلة) — and who frequently boasts (الفخر). Some have said that the meaning of **لَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ** is "do not twist your neck for them in humiliation when in need," but this does not suit the end of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَاقْصِدْ فِي مَشْيِكَ وَاغْضُضْ مِن صَوْتِكَ إِنَّ أَنكَرَ الْأَصْوَاتِ لَصَوْتُ الْحَمِيرِ** — "‘Be modest in your bearing, and lower your voice. Indeed the most ungainly of voices is the donkey’s voice’" (31:19). Moderation (القصد) in a thing is being balanced in it. Lowering (الغض) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is the reduction of the gaze or the voice. Thus, lowering the voice is its reduction and shortening.

The meaning is: Adopt moderation in your walking and reduction and shortening in your voice; indeed, the most disagreeable of voices is the voice of donkeys due to their exaggeration in raising it.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: I heard Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) saying: "Among the major sins (الكبائر) are: being undutiful to parents (عقوق الوالدين), despairing of the spirit of Allah, and feeling secure from the plan of Allah." It has also been narrated: "The greatest of the major sins is polytheism (الشرك) in Allah."

In *al-Faqih*, regarding the rights narrated from the Master of the Worshipers (peace be upon him): "The greatest right of Allah upon you is that you worship Him and do not associate anything with Him. If you do that with sincerity (إخلاص), He makes it incumbent upon Himself to suffice you in the matters of this world and the Hereafter."

He said: "As for the right of your mother, it is that you know she carried you where no one carries anyone, and she gave you of the fruit of her heart what no one gives anyone, and she protected you with all her limbs. She did not mind being hungry while feeding you, being thirsty while giving you drink, being naked while clothing you, being in the sun while shading you, and forsaking sleep for your sake. She protected you from heat and cold so that you might be hers. Indeed, you are not capable of thanking her except by the help and success (توفيق) of Allah."

"As for the right of your father, it is that you know he is your root. For were it not for him, you would not be. So whenever you see in yourself something that pleases you, know that your father is the root of that blessing upon you. So praise Allah and thank Him in proportion to that; and there is no power except by Allah."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: A man came to the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, to whom should I be most dutiful?" He said: "Your mother." He said: "Then who?" He said: "Your mother." He said: "Then who?" He said: "Your mother." He said: "Then who?" He said: "Your father."

In *al-Manaqib*: Al-Husayn ibn Ali (peace be upon him) passed by Abd al-Rahman ibn Amr ibn al-As. Abdullah said: "Whoever would like to look at the most beloved of the people of the earth to the people of heaven, let him look at this man passing by, though I have not spoken to him since the nights of Siffin."

Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri brought him to al-Husayn (peace be upon him), and al-Husayn (peace be upon him) said to him: "Do you know that I am the most beloved of the people of the earth to the people of heaven, yet you fought me and my father on the day of Siffin? By Allah, my father is better than me." He offered an excuse and said: "Indeed, the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) said to me: 'Obey your father.'" Al-Husayn (peace be upon him) said to him: "Have you not heard the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَإِن جَاهَدَاكَ عَلَى أَن تُشْرِكَ بِي مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا** (But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them)? And the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) said: 'Obedience is only in what is right (المعروف),' and his saying: 'There is no obedience to a creature in disobedience to the Creator.'"

In *al-Faqih*, among his (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) concise sayings: **لا طاعة لمخلوق في معصية الخالق** — "There is no obedience to a creature in disobedience to the Creator."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: I heard him saying: "Beware of the belittled sins (المحقرات من الذنوب), for they have a seeker. One of you says: 'I sinned and I seek forgiveness,' while Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَاهُ فِي إِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ** (and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps, and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam) (36:12). And the Mighty and Majestic said: **إِنَّهَا إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُن فِي صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ أَوْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ** (Even if it should be the weight of a mustard seed, and [even if] it should be in a rock, or in the heavens, or in the earth, Allah will produce it. Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware) (31:16)."

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Mu‘awiyah ibn Wahb, who said: I asked Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) about the best thing by which servants draw near to their Lord and the most beloved of that to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. He said: "I do not know of anything after knowledge (المعرفة) better than this prayer." [The narration continues].

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), that he said: **الصلاة قربان كل تقي** — "The prayer is the means of proximity for every God-conscious person."

In *al-Majma‘*: **وَاصْبِرْ عَلَى مَا أَصَابَكَ** (and be patient through whatever may visit you) — of hardship and harm in bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong: narrated from Ali (peace be upon him).

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ** — "That is, do not turn your face away from people in pride, and do not turn away from one who speaks to you out of disdain for him." This meaning is the saying of Ibn Abbas and Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Al-Tabarani, Ibn Adi, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari that the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) was asked about the saying of Allah: **وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ**. He said: "It refers to the corner of the mouth (الشدق)."

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ أَنكَرَ الْأَصْوَاتِ لَصَوْتُ الْحَمِيرِ** — It is narrated from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) that he said: "It is the loud, ugly sneeze, and the man who raises his voice in speech in an ugly manner, unless he is a caller or reciting the Qur’an."

**I say**: Regarding all these meanings, especially concerning undutifulness to parents (العقوق), there are many corroborating narrations.

## (A Discussion on the Story of Luqman and a Selection of His Wisdom, in Two Sections)

1 — The name of Luqman does not appear in His speech, the Exalted, except in Surah Luqman, and nothing of his stories is mentioned except what is in the saying of the Mighty Speaker: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا لُقْمَانَ الْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ** (Certainly We gave Luqman wisdom, [saying], ‘Give thanks to Allah’). Many different narrations have been reported regarding his story and wisdom, and we shall cite some of those that are closer to being reliable.

In *al-Kafi*, from some of our companions, attributed to Hisham ibn al-Hakam, who said: Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said to me: "O Hisham, indeed Allah said: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا لُقْمَانَ الْحِكْمَةَ**." He said: "[It means] understanding and intellect (العقل)."

In *al-Majma‘*, Nafi‘ narrated from Ibn Umar, who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) saying: "Truly I say, Luqman was not a prophet, but he was a servant who reflected much and had excellent certainty (اليقين). He loved Allah, so He loved him and bestowed wisdom upon him."

"He was sleeping at midday when a call came to him: 'O Luqman, would you like Allah to make you a vicegerent (خليفة) on the earth, to judge between people with truth?' He answered the voice: 'If my Lord gives me the choice, I accept well-being (العافية) and do not accept trial (البلاء). But if He mandates it upon me, then I hear and obey, for I know that if He does that to me, He will help me and protect me.'"

"The angels said with a voice, though he did not see them: 'Why, O Luqman?' He said: 'Because judging is the most difficult of stations and the most emphasized; injustice (الظلم) envelops it from every place. If he fulfills it, he is likely to be saved, but if he errs, he misses the path to Paradise. And whoever is lowly in the world but noble in the Hereafter is better than one who is noble in the world but lowly in the Hereafter. And whoever chooses the world over the Hereafter, the world will elude him and he will not attain the Hereafter.'"

"The angels marveled at the beauty of his speech. Then he slept a sleep and was given wisdom, and he woke up speaking with it. Then he used to assist Dawud with his wisdom, and Dawud said to him: 'Blessed are you, O Luqman; you were given wisdom and trial was diverted from you.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: The Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace) said: "Do you know what Luqman was?" They said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "He was an Abyssinian."

2 — In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of narration to Hammad, who said: I asked Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) about Luqman and his wisdom which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, mentioned. He said: "By Allah, Luqman was not given wisdom due to lineage, wealth, family, physical stature, or beauty."

"But he was a man strong in the matter of Allah, God-conscious (متورعًا) in Allah, silent, tranquil, deep in observation, long in reflection, sharp in vision, and self-sufficient through lessons. He never slept during the day. No one ever saw him urinating, defecating, or bathing due to his extreme concealment, the depth of his observation, and his caution in his affairs. He never laughed at anything out of fear of sin. He never became angry, never joked with anyone, never rejoiced over anything of worldly matters that came to him, and never grieved over anything of it. He married women and many children were born to him, and most of them passed away before him, yet he did not weep over the death of any of them."

"He never passed by two men disputing or fighting except that he reconciled them, and he would not leave them until they loved one another. He never heard a saying from anyone that he found good except that he asked for its explanation and from whom he had taken it. He used to frequently sit with the jurists (الفقهاء) and the wise (الحكماء). He used to visit judges, kings, and rulers; he would pity the judges for what they were tested with, and have mercy on the kings and rulers for their delusion regarding Allah and their complacency in that. He would take lessons and learn that by which he could overcome his soul and struggle against his desires (الهوى), and by which he could guard against Satan. He would treat his heart with reflection and treat his soul with lessons. He would not travel except for what concerned him; thus he was given wisdom and granted protection (العصمة)."

"And Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, commanded groups of angels—when it was midday and eyes were quiet during the siesta—to call Luqman where he could hear but not see them. They said: 'O Luqman, would you like Allah to make you a vicegerent on the earth to judge between people?' Luqman said: 'If Allah commands me to do that, then I hear and obey, for if He does that, He will help me, teach me, and protect me. But if He gives me the choice, I accept well-being.' The angels said: 'O Luqman, why?' He said: 'Because judging between people is the most difficult of stations and the most full of trials and tribulations. One is forsaken and not helped, and injustice envelops it from every place. Its practitioner is between two matters: if he hits upon the truth, he is likely to be safe, but if he errs, he misses the path to Paradise. And whoever is lowly and weak in the world, it is easier for him in the Return than to be a noble and eminent judge. And whoever chooses the world over the Hereafter loses them both; this one vanishes and that one is not attained.'"

He said: "The angels marveled at his wisdom, and the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) found his speech good. When evening came and he took to his bed for the night, Allah sent down wisdom upon him, enveloping him with it from his head to his feet while he was sleeping. He covered him with wisdom as with a garment. Then he woke up as the wisest of people in his time, and he went out to the people speaking with wisdom and spreading it among them."

He said: "When he was offered the judgment of vicegerency and did not accept it, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded the angels, and they called Dawud to the vicegerency, and he accepted it without making the condition that Luqman had made. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, gave him the vicegerency on the earth, and he was tested by it more than once, each time falling into error, and Allah would excuse him and forgive him. Luqman used to visit Dawud (peace be upon him) frequently and exhort him with his exhortations, wisdom, and the excellence of his knowledge. Dawud used to say to him: 'Blessed are you, O Luqman; you were given wisdom and trial was diverted from you, while Dawud was given the vicegerency and tested with judgment and trial (الفتنة).'"

Then Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) said regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَانُ لِابْنِهِ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُ يَا بُنَيَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ**: "Luqman exhorted his son with such profound effects that he was deeply moved and shaken."

"And among what he exhorted him with, O Hammad, was that he said: 'O my son, since you fell into the world, you have turned your back to it and faced the Hereafter. A home toward which you are traveling is closer to you than a home from which you are moving away. O my son, sit with the scholars and crowd them with your knees, but do not argue with them lest they deprive you. Take from the world what is sufficient and do not reject it entirely, lest you become a burden on people. But do not enter into it in a way that harms your Hereafter. Fast a fast that cuts your desire, but do not fast a fast that prevents you from prayer, for prayer is more beloved to Allah than fasting.'"

"'O my son, the world is a deep sea in which many people have perished. So make your ship in it faith (الإيمان), make its sail reliance (التوكل) [on Allah], and make your provision in it the God-consciousness (تقوى) of Allah. If you are saved, it is by the mercy of Allah, and if you perish, it is because of your sins.'"

"'O my son, if you learn manners (تأدبت) while young, you will benefit from them when old. Whoever cares for manners becomes concerned with them, and whoever is concerned with them takes the trouble to learn them. Whoever takes the trouble to learn them becomes intense in seeking them, and whoever is intense in seeking them attains their benefit. So take it as a habit, for you will succeed your predecessors and those after you will benefit from it. One who desires will hope in you, and one who fears will dread your power. Beware of laziness in seeking it for the sake of something else. If you are overcome in the world, do not be overcome in the Hereafter. If you miss seeking knowledge in its proper places, you have been overcome in the Hereafter. Make in your days, nights, and hours a portion for seeking knowledge, for you will find no waste more severe than abandoning it. Do not dispute therein with a stubborn person, do not argue with a jurist, do not be an enemy to a ruler, do not walk with an oppressor nor befriend him, and do not brother a profligate (فاسق) nor accompany a suspect. Store your knowledge as you store your wealth.'"

"'O my son, fear Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, with such a fear that if you were to come on the Day of Resurrection with the righteous deeds of all humans and jinn, you would still fear that He might punish you. And hope in Allah with such a hope that if you were to meet Him on the Day of Resurrection with the sins of all humans and jinn, you would still hope that Allah might forgive you.'"

His son said to him: "O father, how can I manage this when I have only one heart?" Luqman said to him: "O my son, if the heart of a believer were extracted, two lights would be found in it: a light for fear and a light for hope. If they were weighed, neither would outweigh the other by the weight of an atom. Whoever believes in Allah believes what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said; and whoever believes what Allah has said does what Allah has commanded. Whoever does not do what Allah has commanded does not believe what Allah has said. For these traits testify for one another."

"Whoever believes in Allah with a sincere faith works for Allah with pure sincerity and counsel. Whoever works for Allah with pure sincerity and counsel has believed in Allah sincerely. Whoever obeys Allah fears Him, and whoever fears Him loves Him, and whoever loves Him follows His command. Whoever follows His command deserves His Paradise and His pleasure. And whoever does not follow the pleasure of Allah, His wrath becomes light to him—we seek refuge in Allah from the wrath of Allah."

"'O my son, do not lean toward the world nor occupy your heart with it, for Allah has not created a creation that is more insignificant to Him than it. Do you not see that He did not make its bliss the reward of the obedient, nor did He make its trial the punishment of the disobedient?'"

In *Qurb al-Isnad*: Harun from Ibn Sadaqah from Ja‘far from his father (peace be upon him): It was said to Luqman: "What is the summary of your wisdom?" He said: "I do not burden myself with what I have been sufficed, and I do not neglect what I have been entrusted."

In *al-Bihar*, from *Qisas al-Anbiya’*, with its chain of narration to Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: Among what Luqman exhorted his son was that he said: "O my son, if you are in doubt about death, then remove sleep from yourself—and you will not be able to do that. And if you are in doubt about the Resurrection (البعث), then remove waking up from yourself—and you will not be able to do that. For if you reflect on this, you will know that your soul is in the hand of another. Sleep is but in the position of death, and waking after sleep is but in the position of resurrection after death." And he said: Luqman said to his son: "O my son, do not draw too close lest you be distanced, and do not stay too far lest you be humiliated. Every beast loves its like, but the son of Adam does not love his like. Do not spread your wares except before one who seeks them. Just as there is no friendship between the ram and the wolf, so there is no friendship between the righteous and the profligate. Whoever draws near to pitch, some of it will stick to him; likewise, whoever partners with the profligate will learn from his ways. Whoever loves disputation will be insulted, whoever enters the places of evil will be suspected, whoever accompanies an evil companion will not be safe, and whoever does not control his tongue will regret."

And he said: "O my son, accompany a hundred but do not make an enemy of one. O my son, it is but your portion (خلاقك) and your character (خلقك). Your portion is your religion, and your character is between you and the people; so do not make yourself hateful to them, and learn the beauties of character."

"O my son, be a servant to the virtuous and do not be a son to the wicked. O my son, fulfill the trust (الأمانة) and your world and Hereafter will be safe. Be trustworthy, for Allah does not love the treacherous. O my son, do not show people that you fear Allah while your heart is profligate."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Yahya ibn Uqbah al-Azdi from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: Among what Luqman exhorted his son was: "O my son, people before you have gathered for their children, but what they gathered did not remain, nor did those for whom they gathered remain. You are but a hired servant who has been commanded to perform a task and promised a wage for it. So fulfill your task and receive your wage. Do not be in this world like a sheep that fell into a green field and ate until it became fat, and its death was in its fatness. Rather, make the world like a bridge over a river that you crossed and left behind, never to return to it again. Destroy it and do not build it, for you were not commanded to build it."

"Know that you will be asked tomorrow, when you stand before Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, about four things: your youth, in what you wore it out; your life, in what you spent it; and your wealth, from where you earned it and on what you spent it. So prepare for that and ready an answer. Do not grieve over what you missed of the world, for the little of the world does not last, and the much of it is not safe from trial. So take your precaution, be diligent in your affair, lift the veil from your face, expose yourself to the favor of your Lord, renew repentance (التوبة) in your heart, and be swift in your departure before you are sought, your decree is fulfilled, and you are prevented from what you desire."

In *al-Bihar*, from *al-Qisas*, with its chain of narration to Hammad from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: Luqman said: "O my son, beware of annoyance, ill-temper, and lack of patience, for no companion can remain steadfast with these traits. Oblige your soul to be tranquil (التؤدة) in your affairs, endure the burdens of your brothers, and make your character good with all people."

"O my son, if you lack that with which you can maintain ties with your kin and bestow favor upon your brothers, then do not lack good character and a cheerful countenance. For whoever has good character is loved by the virtuous and avoided by the wicked. Be content with the portion of Allah so that your life may be pure. If you wish to gather the honor of the world, then cut off your greed for what is in the hands of people. For the prophets and the truthful (الصديقون) only reached what they reached by cutting off their greed."

**I say**: The reports regarding his exhortations are many; we have sufficed with what we have cited for the sake of brevity.
